kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>receive</radical> something edible, arrange it in a <radical>circle</radical> and <radical>boil</radical> it. That way, it’ll <kanji>ripen</kanji> more quickly.

The <radical>thread</radical> radical and <kanji>thread</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.

When a <radical>drunkard</radical> holds a <radical>sword</radical> it <kanji>becomes</kanji> something else. The drunkard doesn't become a swordsman, they're still just a drunkard. But the sword in their hands does.

If you want to <radical>dry</radical> your <radical>horns</radical> you need to lay them <kanji>flat</kanji>. If they aren't flat they'll always be a little damp somewhere and you'll never have a moment's <kanji>peace</kanji>.

You have some <radical>dirt</radical> next to a <radical>pool</radical> that has an extra <radical>drop</radical> of water in it because you need them to be <kanji>equal</kanji>. If there's too much dirt outside the pool and not enough water inside, it's gonna look weird. We can't have that.

Lying on the <radical>ground</radical> is something that looks just like the ground, the number <kanji>One</kanji>. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.
In the <radical>cage</radical> there are some <radical>legs</radical>. They are the legs attached to a <kanji>small animal</kanji>.

How do you make a <radical>hole</radical> nice and <radical>big</radical>? You gotta <kanji>stab</kanji> something in it. Just <kanji>thrust</kanji> it right in there. And make sure your movements are really <kanji>sudden</kanji>. Stabs and thrusts are more effective when they're sudden.
If you scrape a sharp <radical>stone</radical> against your <radical>skin</radical>, it will <kanji>tear</kanji>. The sharp stone tears your skin. It hurts!
The radical for <radical>courage</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>courage</kanji> are exactly the same.
The radical and the kanji are exactly the same, so this means <kanji>someone</kanji>.

This kanji is made up of the ground radical (which also happens to be the kanji for "one") and the radical for two. Combine <radical>one</radical> and <radical>two</radical> together and what do you get? <kanji>Three</kanji>!
In the <radical>cage</radical> there are some <radical>legs</radical>. They are the legs attached to a <kanji>small animal</kanji>.

While riding on your <radical>scooter</radical>, you grow a <radical>mustache</radical> on top of the <radical>mustache</radical> you already have in order to <kanji>surpass</kanji> the people around you. You're in a scooter race and your mustache is the only thing keeping you in the lead, but someone else has a mustache too! So you grow another mustache to surpass them once again!
The radical for <radical>eat</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>eat</kanji> are the same! Lucky you.
If you know the radical meaning <radical>orders</radical> you know the kanji meaning <kanji>orders</kanji> as well!
A <radical>woman</radical> who is a <radical>nurse</radical> should be called <kanji>miss</kanji>.

You're known to get the ailment <radical>evening</radical> <radical>toe</radical>, which is when your toe catches on fire after getting really, really hot (imagine this happening, ouch!). The only way to fix it is to cool your toe down, and the best way to do that is to go <kanji>outside</kanji>.

Aim the <radical>scooter</radical>'s <radical>neck</radical> at the <kanji>road</kanji>! Scooters are only meant for driving on <kanji>street</kanji>s and <kanji>path</kanji>s, and if you go the wrong <kanji>way</kanji>, you'll probably break your own neck!

In a <radical>private</radical> place on the <radical>moon</radical>, you hide two <radical>spoon</radical>s that give you a special <kanji>ability</kanji>. It's the ability to predict the future! The private moon spoons reflect the knowledge of time and space into your brain. Now that's a cool ability!
The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same. Both are <kanji>power</kanji>.

A great <radical>leader</radical> can look <radical>straight</radical> at something and know its <kanji>value</kanji>. That social media site? 44 billion dollars. That job position? 50k a year. A four-day work week? Um… sorry, no, there's no value there. See? Leaders can see straight to the heart of matters to grasp something's true value.
You have a <radical>bow</radical> and you want to make it <radical>long</radical>. To do that, you'd naturally just <kanji>stretch</kanji> it.

By a <radical>tree</radical>, there's a <radical>sheep</radical> in the <radical>water</radical> with a <kanji>formal name ender</kanji>. That means everyone calls him Sheep-sama. No one calls him just "Sheep," since he's kind of a big deal. He's actually like sheep royalty. When he's not in his castle, Sheep-sama enjoys playing in the water by the trees.

This can also mean <kanji>manner</kanji>, as in your manner of doing things. I guess someone with a formal name ender has a different manner of doing things.

meaning: massage (esp. anma, a traditional form of Japanese massage)

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: When your <radical>sick</radical> <radical>mama</radical> tries to do a <radical>task</radical> she's filled with <kanji>pain</kanji>!

See that <radical>gold</radical> man <radical>oversee</radical>ing everyone? He's a <kanji>model</kanji> human. His goldenness is a sign of how exemplary he is, and he's overseeing all of us flawed, regular folks to try to help us get on his level.
You build a <radical>roof</radical> over your <radical>coat rack</radical> because you <kanji>determine</kanji> that this is the best way to protect your coats from the elements.
The <radical>stone</radical> radical and <kanji>stone</kanji> kanji are the same thing!

This man with a gun is headed your direction and he doesn’t even know what you and your tribe are gonna do to him. If he gets any closer, you’re likely to shoot that arrow right at him.
What does your <radical>mama</radical> tell you to do in the <radical>street</radical>? Look before you cross! You gotta do it <kanji>beforehand</kanji>, or it could be too late.

Only a <radical>tree</radical> with <radical>eye</radical>s and a <radical>heart</radical> has the ability to <kanji>imagine</kanji> and to <kanji>contemplate</kanji>. For a tree to imagine and contemplate things, it needs eyes to see and a heart to feel.

There's a <radical>scooter</radical> with your <radical>mama</radical> carrying a <radical>task</radical> she needs to get done. You see her <kanji>pass through</kanji>, hurrying by to get that task done.
<radical>Tsunami</radical>s in the <radical>past</radical> changed the <kanji>method</kanji>s and <kanji>law</kanji>s of today.

See that <radical>gold</radical> man <radical>oversee</radical>ing everyone? He's a <kanji>model</kanji> human. His goldenness is a sign of how exemplary he is, and he's overseeing all of us flawed, regular folks to try to help us get on his level.
You build a <radical>roof</radical> over your <radical>coat rack</radical> because you <kanji>determine</kanji> that this is the best way to protect your coats from the elements.

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits a <radical>tree</radical> and your <radical>hat</radical> protects you from the <radical>water</radical>, but the hat itself is now covered in <kanji>lacquer</kanji>. You take it off and see that it's shiny and hard and protected.

The <radical>original</radical> radical and the <kanji>original</kanji> kanji are the same!

This kanji can also mean <kanji>field</kanji>, probably because that's what everything originally looked like before humans: just a bunch of fields.

There is a line on the <radical>ground</radical> so you <radical>stop</radical>, making a giant sign pop up that says <kanji>CORRECT</kanji>! You obeyed the rules of the road and did the correct thing. Good on you.

Let the <radical>sun</radical> shine into your <radical>ear</radical> while sitting on a <radical>stool</radical> and make sure <kanji>most</kanji> of it gets in your head. You want to fill your brain with the most sun anyone ever has.

There's a <radical>mouth</radical> inside a <radical>mouth</radical>. But if you look even closer (not actually, but we're pretending), you can see another mouth inside that mouth... and another one inside of that one! It keeps going and going. Can you count the number of <kanji>times</kanji> there's a mouth inside a mouth? This kanji also means <kanji>revolve</kanji> too. Think of each "time" a thing happens, it loops back around and comes back to the beginning, revolving around again another time.
The <radical>moon</radical> is the <radical>same</radical> as your <kanji>torso</kanji>. You must have a really huge torso for it to be the same as the moon.

The <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>book</radical> all about the human <kanji>body</kanji>. Knowing about bodies is a privilege. One that only leaders get—it helps them control all their subjects, like you.

Your <radical>rice</radical> <radical>fortune</radical> is <kanji>sticky</kanji>. Rice is sticky. So is your rice fortune. Your fortune of rice is sticky because it is made up entirely of sticky rice.

A <radical>horn</radical>ed <radical>king</radical> on a <radical>slide</radical> catches your <radical>eye</radical> because you love what he's chosen to <kanji>wear</kanji>. You can't wait for him to <kanji>arrive</kanji> at the bottom of the slide so you can check out his outfit close-up.

Everyone has a <radical>soul</radical> in their <radical>life</radical>, and that soul shapes their <kanji>gender</kanji>. It's not just about body parts — it's about what's inside. In the end, it's one's <kanji> nature</kanji> that truly matters.

When your <radical>self</radical> goes down a <radical>slide</radical> and hits a <radical>barb</radical>, it's a painful reminder that you have a <kanji>body</kanji>. Sometimes people think their self is just some kind of floating, disembodied consciousness, but getting a barb stuck in <kanji>oneself</kanji> while going down a slide will quickly remind one of one's bodily existence.

The <radical>eye</radical> radical and <kanji>eye</kanji> kanji are the same as each other, so now you know them both.

A <radical>tsunami</radical> that is <radical>white</radical>, traveling in the <radical>direction</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> is a <kanji>fierce</kanji> and <kanji>violent</kanji> force of nature. When your <radical>sick</radical> <radical>mama</radical> tries to do a <radical>task</radical> she's filled with <kanji>pain</kanji>!

If you live in the <radical>now</radical>, there’s a good chance your <radical>heart</radical> controls your <kanji>thought</kanji> process. You don’t have time to think with your brain, you need an answer now so you use your heart to make decisions and thought process.

If you want to save it you better <kanji>cover</kanji> up the black hole! The only way to save it from capsizing is to cover it right up!

You fly <radical>north</radical> to the <radical>moon</radical> and break your <kanji>back</kanji> when you finally land on it. You weren’t braced for impact so when you hit the moon, your back took all of the impact.

If you <radical>loiter</radical> around to <radical>poop</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> you never know what could be happening <kanji>behind</kanji> you. <kanji>After</kanji> you're done you could turn <kanji>back</kanji> to see something really crazy that you missed because you took so long to poop in the cold.
